title: Correlation-driven tunability of altermagnetism in RuO$_2$
authors: Ina Park, Dongwook Kim, Jisook Hong, Beomjoon Goh, Bo Gyu Jang
abstract: RuO$_2$ has been regarded as a prototypical candidate for metallic altermagnet, offering a potential platform for high-speed and high-efficiency spintronics. However, the magnetic ground state of RuO$_2$ remains a topic of active debate due to conflicting experimental reports. In this work, we investigate the effect of electron correlations in RuO$_2$ using density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT). In contrast to previous DFT-based studies, DFT+DMFT captures essential dynamical correlation effects, yielding spectral functions and optical conductivities in excellent quantitative agreement with experiments, and further reveals that RuO$_2$ resides in the close vicinity of both the paramagnetic-altermagnetic phase boundary and the itinerant-localized crossover, rendering the magnetic ground state highly susceptible to external perturbations. Indeed, even a minimal compressive strain of $\sim$0.5% is sufficient to drive the system into an altermagnetic phase. These findings elucidate the origin of the conflicting experimental observations and reveal that dynamical correlation effects are the key driving force behind the highly tunable magnetic ground state of RuO$_2$.
summary: This paper uses DFT+DMFT to show that dynamical electron correlations are essential for understanding the magnetic properties of RuO2. It demonstrates that the material's magnetic ground state is highly tunable, with minimal strain being enough to induce altermagnetism. This provides a theoretical resolution to conflicting experimental observations of the material's magnetism.
main_problem: Resolving the conflicting experimental reports regarding whether the magnetic ground state of RuO2 is paramagnetic or altermagnetic and understanding the role of dynamical electron correlations in its tunability.
main_result: The study reveals that RuO2 is highly susceptible to external perturbations, where even a 0.5% compressive strain can drive the system into an altermagnetic phase due to dynamical correlation effects.
method: The researchers used Density Functional Theory combined with Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DFT+DMFT) with a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CTQMC) impurity solver.
model_or_system: The physical system is RuO2, a 4d transition-metal oxide with a rutile structure, characterized as a Hund's metal near the paramagnetic-altermagnetic and itinerant-localized phase boundaries.
key_observables: Spectral functions, optical conductivity, effective mass, quasiparticle weight (Z), ordered magnetic moment (M), and local spin susceptibility.
important_parameters: Coulomb interaction U = 3.5 eV, Hund's coupling J = 0.6 eV, and compressive strain along the c-axis.
main_assumptions: The study assumes a density-density form of the Coulomb interaction using Slater parametrization and focuses on the local approximation inherent in DMFT.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure, ARPES intensity, and spectral functions; Figure 4 compares DFT and DFT+DMFT spectral functions and optical conductivity; Figure 5 discusses orbital-specific effects.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental conflict in RuO2, presents the DFT+DMFT methodology, compares results with experimental ARPES and optical data, investigates the effect of strain on magnetic stability, and concludes with the role of correlations in phase tunability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of rutile RuO2. (Top) Pristine crystal structure without strain. (Bottom) Structure with compressive strain along c direction. (b) Experimental ARPES intensity map overlaid with NM DFT band structure (blue lines) (c) PM DFT+DMFT spectral function A(k, ω) with DFT bands (blue lines). (Right panel) the Ru dx2−y2-projected momentum-integrated spectral function A(ω). (d-e) Interband optical conductivity σ1,inter(ω) for (d) E ∥a and (e) E ⊥a polarization directions, comparing experiment [20], DFT+DMFT (red), and DFT (blue dashed).

FIG. 2. DFT+DMFT U −J phase diagram for (a) the energy difference between the PM and AM phase, ∆E = EPM −EAM (meV/f.u.), (b) ordered moment M (µB/Ru). The black solid line indicates the phase boundary between PM and AM phase. (c) Quasiparticle weight Z of Ru dx2−y2 orbital. The itinerant to localized crossover is guided by the grey dashed line. (d) local spin susceptibility χ0 S vs. T for pristine (square) and 0.5% strained (s = 0.995) (triangle) RuO2 with chosen interaction parameters (U, J) = (3.5, 0.6) (eV) and pristine RuO2 in localized limit with (U, J) = (4.5, 0.8) (circle). Purple star marks the chosen interaction parameter used for the comparison between DFT+DMFT calculation and...

FIG. 3. Strain-dependent properties of RuO2 obtained from DFT (blue) and DFT+DMFT (red). (a) Ordered magnetic moment of Ru atom as a function of strain. (b) Quasiparticle weight Z of Ru dx2−y2 orbital as a function of strain. (c) Partial density of states (PDOS) of the flat band orbital (dx2−y2) as a function of strain, with the upper and lower panels showing the NM DFT and PM DFT+DMFT results, respectively. Darker colors correspond to larger compressive strain. (d,e) Spin-resolved spectral function A(k, ω) obtained from AM DFT+DMFT calculations for (d) zero strain s = 1.0 and (e) finite compressive strain s = 0.995. Red and blue marks spin-up (up) and spin-down (dn) components,...

FIG. 4. (a) DFT and DFT+DMFT spectral function in the extended energy window. Red arrows indicate interband transitions with energy separation of 1.3 eV, corresponding to the prominent peak observed in the NM DFT optical conductivity (Fig. 1(d) and (e)). (b) Interband optical conductivity from AM DFT+DMFT calculation (dashed) with ordered moment M ∼0.1µB/Ru compared with PM DFT+DMFT (thin red) and experimental result (thick black) [20].
FIG. 5. DFT+DMFT orbital-projected band structure of Ru1 atom in the case of pristine RuO2. Refer the section note for orbital nomenclature.
